---
title: ""
format: html
editor: visual
navbar: false
---

## Week 8 - Wednesday, March 18th

### Class Goals

1.  Review understanding predictive statistics (patterns in the data) and evaluate the Big Five and Myers-Briggs personality measures.
2.  Look over some Dig Deeper projects and examples.
3.  Students all learning; Professor not rushing; we all enriched and nourished by knowledge of research methods.

:::::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
### Announcements and Agenda

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
-   **Spring Break Next Week.** Hooray.
-   **Um.** Yeah.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
-   **11:10 - 11:50.** Check-In and Review (Personality).
-   **11:50 - 12:10.** Defining a Likert Scale.
-   **12:10 - 12:35.** Dig Deeper (Discussion 7)
:::
:::::
::::::

### Class Slides

```{=html}
<iframe data-external="1" class="slide-deck" src="/rm/slides/8_patterns.html" width="100%" height="500px" title="" border="5px"></iframe>
```

### Useful Links

-   [CHECK-IN : valid, reliable, and biased personality](https://forms.gle/g2Mfxiobu7qW1BgW9)
-   [Dig Deeper Assignment](/rm/notes/digdeeper.qmd)
-   [Vision Board](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/14u3w5edvo6KSFRw2U9t1knDANFSGBWOvDjS7-ZGujos/edit?usp=sharing)
-   [Personality Profile](/rm/readings/Personality.qmd) \[making time\]

## Before Next Class

### Readings for Next Week (and our Quiz)

1.  [**Four Reasons Why Patterns Occur in the Data \[10-minutes\]**](https://youtu.be/2plZk2tQIQ8)
2.  **Review the [Meyer et al. (2001) article](https://www.dropbox.com/s/r0uwvdqezzvlvdb/2001_Meyer_EffectSizes.pdf?dl=0) on effect sizes.** Identify a psychological relationship from Table 2 that you think is interesting / have heard about in other classes. Write this relationship out as a model. Then, report the correlation coefficient (from the table) for this relationship. Use the “[correlation visualizer](https://rpsychologist.com/correlation/)” to illustrate what this relationship might look like, and [paste a screenshot](https://www.take-a-screenshot.org/) of this relationship as part of your answer. Does this seem like a strong or weak relationship to you? What other factors might explain this relationship? Apply the “four reasons” framework to evaluate why this relationship might occur. [**Here’s a video professor recorded**](https://www.loom.com/share/3228d91e431e4be4b3f1c871df7f1db3?sid=0f022c43-6667-447e-b63c-7be544e9643b) walking through what I’m asking y’all to do since it's a lot of steps and I'm predicting I rushed in class :)
3.  [**Review / Scan / Ignore the Textbook Chapter on Correlations**](https://kpu.pressbooks.pub/psychmethods4e/chapter/correlational-research/)**.** They are patterns in the data.

### Week 8 Discussion Questions.

1.  Will go here.
2.  Professor is tired.
3.  Maybe we won't have discussion questions?
4.  But we probably will.
5.  The learning must continue.
